problem.value: Managers need to correct or update contractual documents after signature, without a full re-issuance from scratch — but the underlying trigger (data error vs. genuine contract change vs. renegotiation) is not stated.
problem.evidence: 'we'd like managers to edit and re-approve documents after they've already been signed'

current_process.evidence: No mention of how post-signature changes are handled today (manual re-signature outside system? not possible at all?).

actors.value: Managers edit and re-approve; unclear if manager is also the sole approver or if a separate/higher approval level is required.

business_objects.value: 'Documents' likely means Contracts (possibly Invoices or other signed artifacts) — but the platform models Contract and Amendment as distinct entities with distinct lifecycles.
business_objects.evidence: Domain notes: Contract (draft→signed→active→ended) vs Amendment (draft→issued) are separate objects.

business_rules.value: Unclear which fields/conditions may be edited post-signature, whether there's a time window, and whether the rule differs by contract type or client.
business_rules.evidence: Context: 'modify the contract' almost always means 'issue an amendment', not patch a field.

workflow.value: Likely: editing a signed document should spawn a new versioned amendment (draft→issued) that reopens signature, rather than an in-place edit of the signed original.
workflow.evidence: Domain notes: editing an issued amendment is 'a new versioned document, not an edit... re-opens signature and can re-trigger downstream automation.'

integrations.evidence: Signature is an event other modules subscribe to (invoicing, notifications); re-approval after edit could re-fire these — risk of duplicate downstream actions.

permissions.value: Managers can edit; approver identity, and whether HR/admin also need edit/view rights, is undefined.
permissions.evidence: Only 'managers' named; approval circuits are typically multi-level and client-specific in this platform.

config_vs_custom.evidence: Approval circuits are configurable per client in this platform; unclear if re-approval circuit should be too.

constraints.value: Signed contracts have legal weight (French labor law); modifying them post-signature likely requires a traceable, versioned document rather than silent editing.
constraints.evidence: Context: French labor law / GDPR on HR data; invoicing follows legal numbering rules.

edge_cases.evidence: Not addressed: concurrent edits, rejection of re-approval, retroactive effect, documents already downstream-processed (e.g., invoiced).

reporting.value: Audit trail of who edited/approved what and when is very likely required given contractual/legal nature of the documents.
reporting.evidence: impact_signals: regulatory/legal documents + re-approval implies a compliance-grade change history is needed, not just a status field.

risks.evidence: Not stated — main risk is duplicate/incorrect downstream triggers (invoicing, notifications) upon re-approval.

questions.q: When you say 'documents,' are these Contracts, or could they also include Invoices or other signed artifacts? And should an edit-after-signature produce a new Amendment (versioned, re-signed) rather than modify the original in place?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: the platform treats Contract and Amendment as distinct entities with different lifecycles — this decision defines the entire data model and build, not just a UI edit.

questions.q: Once a document is signed, other processes (invoicing, notifications, other systems) may already be subscribed to that signature event. If a manager edits and re-approves, should those downstream processes re-fire, stay untouched, or be manually reconciled?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: re-triggering signature-linked automation risks duplicate invoices/notifications; this determines idempotency handling and error recovery design.

questions.q: Who re-approves the edited document — the same manager who edited it, a higher-level approver, or does this depend on client/contract type?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: self-approval vs. multi-level circuit changes the approval workflow shape and whether it needs to be configurable per client.

questions.q: Are there restrictions on what can be edited post-signature (e.g., only certain fields, within a time window, only for certain contract states), or can any signed field be reopened?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: unrestricted editing of a legally signed contract is a very different (and riskier) build than a bounded correction workflow.

questions.q: Is a full audit trail (who changed what, when, previous vs. new values) required for these edits, e.g. for legal/labor-law compliance?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: legal documents under French labor law likely require traceable history, which is a distinct build (versioning + audit log) rather than a simple status change.

summary.objective: Allow managers to modify a signed document and have it re-approved, while preserving legal validity and traceability of the change.
summary.scope: Likely involves introducing (or reusing) an Amendment-style versioned lifecycle on top of the signed Contract: editing spawns a new draft version, which goes through re-approval/re-signature, with the original signed version preserved for audit purposes. May require rules on what/when can be edited, updated permissions for approval, and safeguards on downstream systems (invoicing, notifications) that already reacted to the original signature.
assumptions: 'Documents' refers to Contracts (possibly extending to Amendments), not Invoices.
Editing after signature should create a new versioned document (amendment-style) rather than mutate the signed original in place.
An audit trail of edits/approvals is required due to the legal/contractual nature of the documents.
Downstream automation tied to the original signature event needs explicit handling (re-fire, suppress, or reconcile) to avoid duplication.
summary.blind_spot: The request sounds like a simple 'allow edit + re-approve' toggle, but in this platform a signed Contract is not meant to be edited in place — it's meant to be amended via a new versioned document that reopens signature and can re-trigger every downstream process (invoicing, notifications, other integrations) that subscribed to the original signature. Without clarifying this, the team risks building a silent edit that breaks legal traceability and double-fires automation.
